event_name: UFC Fight Night: Song vs. Figueiredo

fights.fighter_a_bio: Song Yadong throws volume striking combos with sharp footwork, pressuring opponents into the fence where he mixes in clinch knees and takedowns. Vegas has him as the favorite because he's proven he can pace opponents over 15 minutes, but his chin gets tested whenever he gets caught clean.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Figueiredo is a submission specialist with slick transitions—he hunts the guillotine and rear-naked choke by mixing wrestling entries with scrambles off his back. Our model gives Song a 20% edge despite Figueiredo's elite grappling because Song's distance management and cardio can keep this standing.
fights.watch_for: Whether Song can stuff Figueiredo's takedown attempts and avoid scrambles—if this stays on the feet beyond round 2, Song's pressure wins; if Figueiredo drags it to the mat, submission threat becomes real.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Zhang Mingyang is a heavy-handed striker who builds pressure with kicks and uses distance control to land clean combinations. He's a massive -230 favorite (92.6% win probability) because his Elo rating proves he dismantles opponents who can't match his pace and precision.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Alonzo Menifield has the grappling and clinch work to disrupt strikers, plus a higher Elo (1582.8) that reflects hard wins—but he's a +188 underdog here because Zhang's footwork keeps threats at bay. His path to victory is forcing exchanges where wrestling matters, not trading at range.
fights.watch_for: Whether Menifield can close the distance and drag Zhang into the clinch in rounds 2-3, or if Zhang's superior striking positioning keeps the fight at his preferred range where Menifield has no answer.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Pavlovich is a heavyweight wrecking ball who loads up on every punch—his right hand has finished opponents in seconds. He's vulnerable to pressure and leg kicks early, but if he plants his feet and lands clean, fights end fast.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Teixeira throws volume combinations and stays mobile, using footwork to avoid getting cornered. He lacks one-punch KO power and will need to accumulate damage over rounds, which is hard against someone who finishes in bursts.
fights.watch_for: Whether Teixeira can keep moving and land leg kicks in the first two rounds to slow Pavlovich down—if Sergei gets set and starts swinging freely, the fight likely ends.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Kai Asakura is a big favorite (-307) because he controls fights through constant forward pressure and clinch work, grinding opponents down over three rounds. His Elo rating (1470.8) reflects proven success, but he can be caught trading hands at range—his volume striking has openings.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Cameron Smotherman has a higher Elo (1486.1) on paper but Vegas sees him as a +246 underdog, meaning oddsmakers expect Asakura's pressure to overwhelm him. Smotherman will need to catch Asakura early with counter shots or submission attempts before getting pinned against the cage.
fights.watch_for: Can Smotherman keep the fight at range in round one, or does Asakura clinch and control from the opening bell?

fights.fighter_a_bio: Salikhov is a big favorite here because he's a precision striker who uses distance control and quick combinations to wear opponents down, especially over the middle rounds. He'll look to cut angles and land clean shots before retreating—risky if he gets caught, but it's won him 68% of the time in our model.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Matthews relies on forward pressure and clinch work to smother strikers and create scrambles where he can wrestle or land knees. He's hunting for volume and ring control, but that aggression can leave him exposed to counters if Salikhov catches him walking in.
fights.watch_for: Whether Matthews can close the distance and get his hands on Salikhov early—if he eats clean strikes while advancing, he'll be chasing the whole fight; if he lands a clinch and controls the pace, the gap closes fast.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Perez pressures with heavy hands and looks to drag fights into the clinch where he controls pace with knees and elbows. He's the Vegas favorite (+139 odds) because his grappling and cardio have proven more consistent at this level.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Sumudaerji counters with sharp footwork and uses distance to land combinations before resetting away from the fence. He's dangerous when he controls range, but tends to fade when forced into prolonged exchanges.
fights.watch_for: If Perez closes the distance and secures the clinch in rounds 2-3, Sumudaerji's gas tank gets tested and Perez's knees take over. If Sumudaerji stays on the outside and makes Perez chase, he can rack up points and avoid the grind.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Yi Sak Lee is a pure striker who lives in the pocket, throwing volume combinations and relying on hand speed to overwhelm opponents. Vulnerable to wrestlers and grapplers who can shut down his offense at range.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Luis Felipe Dias is Vegas's slight favorite, likely because he mixes takedowns with heavy striking to control pace. He'll dictate where the fight happens—stand with strikers, wrestle grapplers.
fights.watch_for: Can Lee keep the fight standing and at distance? If Dias shoots takedowns early and lands them, he'll grind out a decision. If Lee forces the pocket firefight, his speed could win rounds.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Ding Meng is operating at dead even on our model with minimal historical data to lean on. This is a coin flip until we see how he handles real UFC-level pressure.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Jose Henrique sits at the same 50-50 split with an identical Elo rating, meaning our system has no edge either direction. Both fighters are unproven enough that Vegas isn't even posting a line.
fights.watch_for: Whoever controls the clinch and dictates where the fight happens in round one—that's usually the tell when two unknowns meet, since wrestling and cage control often separate the next tier up from the regional circuit.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Aoriqileng lands hard, straight combinations down the middle and isn't afraid to trade in the pocket—he walks through jabs to get inside where his power shots do real damage. Our model likes him here because he's proven he can hurt strikers and drag the fight into his range, but Vegas has him as a heavy underdog, suggesting they see something different.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Haddon's the Vegas pick and a slight favorite on paper despite lower recent activity—he's built a career on technical boxing fundamentals and footwork to stay on the outside, making opponents miss before countering. He's a cerebral fighter who controls distance, but Aoriqileng's pressure and willingness to eat shots to close gaps is exactly the style that makes distance-dependent strikers uncomfortable.
fights.watch_for: Whether Haddon can keep his feet away and snap counters while Aoriqileng walks in—if Aoriqileng closes the gap early, he'll start landing combinations; if Haddon keeps moving and makes him miss for two rounds, the momentum shifts.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Rei Tsuruya is a big favorite (-340) because he hunts takedowns early and rides top position to wear opponents down—he's methodical and hard to escape once he's got you pinned. His Elo (1501.8) reflects a fighter who executes the same gameplan reliably, which works until someone stuffs his shots.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Jesus Aguilar (Elo 1514.5) has faced better competition overall, but he's the underdog here because Tsuruya's wrestling directly counters what he does best—likely striking or clinch work off the feet. He needs to either keep distance or threaten submissions to discourage takedown attempts.
fights.watch_for: Can Aguilar stay vertical and land strikes before Tsuruya closes distance for a takedown? If Tsuruya gets on top early, the fight's likely his; if Aguilar survives the first-round pressure standing, his higher fight IQ (better Elo) gives him a real shot in rounds 2-3.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Angela Hill is a volume striker who wins rounds by landing a high pace of kicks and hands, but relies on point accumulation rather than finishing power. She can get caught by patient counters and struggles when opponents plant their feet and trade.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Xiong Jingnan dominates with relentless pressure wrestling and heavy clinch work, using her size and strength to control opponents against the cage. She's vulnerable to precise strikers who can land clean and create distance before getting dragged into her grappling range.
fights.watch_for: Whether Hill can maintain her distance and keep Xiong off her with leg kicks, or if Xiong closes the gap early and forces Hill into the clinch where Xiong's strength takes over.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Zhu Kangjie is a near-even matchup with minimal track record to draw from—his Elo sits right at baseline. Without clear fight film, he's a blank slate, which means expect adjustments mid-fight rather than a preset game plan.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Ramon Taveras is virtually identical on paper: same win probability, same uncertainty, same lack of proven résumé depth. This is a coin flip built on incomplete information.
fights.watch_for: Whoever imposes their gameplan first round wins—look for early clinch control, takedown success, or consistent striking patterns to separate them, since neither has established tendencies you can bank on.

fights.fighter_a_bio: Loma Lookboonmee is the Vegas favorite despite our model giving her only a 7.7% win probability—a major red flag that either the betting market knows something our data doesn't, or she's being overvalued. She mixes striking and grappling but hasn't built the track record to justify favorite status against Amorim.
fights.fighter_b_bio: Jaqueline Amorim is a heavy underdog in Vegas despite our model favoring her 92.3%—she likely dominates through clinch control and heavy top pressure, methodically breaking opponents down rather than hunting finishes. If you're seeing +114 odds on her, this is where the app's edge comes in.
fights.watch_for: If Loma gets taken down in round 1, Amorim's top control and ground-and-pound will be nearly impossible to escape—Loma's low Elo rating suggests she hasn't faced this type of relentless pressure before.
